did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used Deep Learning (DL) models, specifically EfficientNetB0, EfficientNetB1, and EfficientNetB3, combined with data sampling techniques like Random Over Sampling (ROS) and SMOTE, to classify skin lesions (including Melanoma, Nevus, BCC, SCC, ACK, and SEV) from smartphone images using the PAD-UFES-20 dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the training and evaluation of Deep Learning models (EfficientNetB0, EfficientNetB1, EfficientNetB3) on the PAD-UFES-20 dataset to classify skin lesions. It reports specific performance metrics (accuracy, F1-score, AUC) for two distinct classification scenarios and compares these results with state-of-the-art methods, which characterizes it as an original technical study rather than a review.

title: DL Methods for Skin Lesions Automated Diagnosis In Smartphone Images
journal: 2023 International Wireless Communications and Mobile Computing (IWCMC)
brief_summary: This study addresses the automated diagnosis of skin lesions from smartphone images using Deep Learning models to improve early detection of skin cancer. The authors utilized the PAD-UFES-20 dataset, which contains 2,298 imbalanced smartphone images of six skin lesion classes, and applied data sampling techniques like Random Over Sampling (ROS) and SMOTE to handle class imbalance. The research evaluated two scenarios: a six-class classification using EfficientNetB0 with ROS, and a binary triage between Melanoma and Nevus using EfficientNetB1 with ROS. The six-class model achieved an accuracy of 92.01% and an AUC of 97.12%, while the binary classification model reached an accuracy of 98.98% and an AUC of 99.95%. These results demonstrate that the proposed DL methods are highly effective for skin lesion classification and have potential utility for clinicians in smartphone-based diagnosis.

all_task_types.target: Six skin lesion classes (Basal Cell Carcinoma, Melanoma, Squamous Cell Carcinoma, Actinic Keratosis, Nevus, Seborrheic Keratosis)
all_task_types.details: Scenario I: Multiclass classification of six skin lesion types from smartphone images using the PAD-UFES-20 dataset. The best model (EfficientNetB0 with ROS) achieved an accuracy of 92.01%, F1-score of 92.01%, and AUC of 97.12%.

all_task_types.target: Two skin lesion classes (Melanoma and Nevus)
all_task_types.details: Scenario II: Binary classification (triage) between Melanoma and Nevus. The best model (EfficientNetB1 with ROS) achieved an accuracy of 98.98%, F1-score of 99.22%, and AUC of 99.95%.

datasets_used.name: PAD-UFES-20
datasets_used.description: A skin lesion dataset composed of patient data and clinical images collected from smartphones in collaboration with the Dermatological and Surgical Assistance Program (PAD) at the Federal University of the Espirito Santo (UFES) in Brazil. It contains 2,298 cases belonging to six distinct skin lesions categories, including clinical information such as age, gender, Fitzpatrick skin type, and lesion diameter.

use.training: Used for training Deep Learning models (EfficientNetB0, EfficientNetB1, EfficientNetB3, etc.) in two scenarios: Scenario I (six-class classification) and Scenario II (binary classification of Melanoma and Nevus). Data sampling techniques (ROS, SMOTE) were applied to handle class imbalance.
use.validation: Used for validation to evaluate model performance and determine optimal parameters.
use.testing: Used for testing to assess the final performance metrics (accuracy, F1-score, AUC) of the trained models.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To handle class imbalance in the dataset by generating synthetic samples for minority classes.
To handle class imbalance by creating more varied and representative samples in minority classes, reducing overfitting risk.
To improve algorithm convergence, reduce complexity, and eliminate the effect of dominant variables by normalizing feature values.
To evaluate and compare the performance of Deep Learning models in classifying skin lesions.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, F1-score, and AUC metrics, and compared against state-of-the-art methods without statistical significance testing.

Scenario I (Six-class classification: BC.notes: EfficientNetB0 with ROS achieved the highest accuracy and F1-score. EfficientNetB2 with ROS achieved the highest AUC. All models exceeded an AUC of 0.9494. EfficientNetB0 + ROS and EfficientNetB3 + SMOTE scored 1.0 for the Melanoma class F1-score.

Scenario II (Binary classification: Mela.metric: Accuracy, F1-score, AUC, Loss, Precision, Recall
Scenario II (Binary classification: Mela.notes: EfficientNetB1 + ROS outperformed EfficientNetB0 + ROS. The proposed method's accuracy (0.9898) was slightly lower than Medhat et al [20] (0.99155), but had higher F1-score (0.9922 vs 0.97661) and better recall/precision balance.

architecture_used.main_model: EfficientNetB0, EfficientNetB1, EfficientNetB2, EfficientNetB3, ResNet50V2, Xception, MobileNetV2, DenseNet121, DenseNet169
architecture_used.pretraining: Transfer Learning (pre-trained weights implied by use of standard DL models for classification)

architecture_details.backbone: Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) including ResNets, Xceptions, MobileNets, DenseNets, and EfficientNets

backbone_details.ResNets: Use residual connections to solve vanishing gradients
backbone_details.Xceptions: Use depthwise separable convolutions and 'Extreme Inception' architecture
backbone_details.MobileNets: Lightweight CNNs using inverted residuals and linear bottlenecks
backbone_details.DenseNets: Use dense connections between layers for feature reuse
backbone_details.EfficientNets: Optimized for efficiency and accuracy using compound scaling strategy, depthwise separable convolutions, and squeeze-and-excitation blocks

training_details.learning_rate: 10^-3 for epochs <= 10; lr * e^-0.1 for epochs > 10
training_details.data_sampling: Random Over Sampling (ROS) and SMOTE (Synthetic Minority Over-sampling Technique)

classification_or_output_head.Scenario_I: Dense (6, Softmax) for 6 classes (BCC, MEL, SCC, ACK, NEV, SEK)

preprocessing_techniques: Data Standardization (normalizing feature values using mean and standard deviation)
Data Splitting (80% for training/validation, 20% for testing; 90/10 split within training set)
Data Sampling for class imbalance (Random Over Sampling (ROS) and SMOTE)
Data Augmentation (mentioned in related works context, e.g., Ali et al., but not explicitly detailed as applied by the authors in their own methodology section beyond sampling)

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied to balance class distribution by duplicating existing minority class samples. Used in Scenario I (EfficientNetB0, Xception, MobileNetV2, DenseNet121, DenseNet169, ResNet50V2, EfficientNetB1, EfficientNetB2) and Scenario II (EfficientNetB1, EfficientNetB0).

Applied to balance class distribution by generating new synthetic class samples through interpolation between existing minority class samples. Used in Scenario I (EfficientNetB3) and compared against ROS in other models.

conclusions: Deep Learning models, specifically EfficientNetB0 with Random Over Sampling (ROS), achieved high performance in classifying six skin lesion types (Basal Cell Carcinoma, Melanoma, Squamous Cell Carcinoma, Actinic Keratosis, Nevus, and Seborrheic Keratosis) from smartphone images, reaching an accuracy of 92.01%, F1-score of 92.01%, and AUC of 97.12%.

For binary triage between Melanoma and Nevus, EfficientNetB1 with ROS demonstrated superior performance with an accuracy of 98.98%, F1-score of 99.22%, and AUC of 99.95%, outperforming state-of-the-art methods in terms of F1-score and recall/precision balance despite a slightly lower accuracy than Medhat et al.

The study confirms the capability and efficiency of using smartphone images for melanoma detection and skin lesion classification, suggesting these methods have potential utility as tools for clinicians to enhance healthcare quality and early diagnosis.

Data sampling techniques such as ROS and SMOTE were effective in handling the class imbalance inherent in the PAD-UFES-20 dataset, with ROS generally yielding better or comparable results to SMOTE across the tested models.

EfficientNetB0 + ROS and EfficientNetB3 + SMOTE achieved perfect F1-scores (1.0) for the Melanoma class in the six-class classification scenario, highlighting their effectiveness in detecting this critical cancer type.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the PAD-UFES-20 dataset, which contains only 2,298 smartphone images; the authors explicitly acknowledge that more research on other datasets is needed to validate generalization efficiency.

The dataset exhibits significant class imbalance, necessitating the use of data sampling techniques (ROS and SMOTE) rather than standard data augmentation, which may introduce bias or overfitting risks associated with synthetic or duplicated samples.
No external validation was performed; the models were evaluated only on the internal test split of the PAD-UFES-20 dataset, limiting the assessment of real-world clinical applicability.
The paper does not report formal statistical hypothesis testing, p-values, or confidence intervals to support the significance of performance differences between models.
The input image resolution is very low (75x75x3 pixels), which may limit the extraction of fine-grained diagnostic features compared to higher-resolution dermoscopic images.

The study focuses solely on image data and does not incorporate the clinical metadata (age, gender, Fitzpatrick skin type, lesion diameter) available in the PAD-UFES-20 dataset, potentially missing valuable diagnostic context.
Deployment concerns are noted as future work; the current study does not address computational efficiency, latency, or integration into actual mobile or web applications for clinical use.
